html:
Louise Alexandra Marie Irene of Battenberg, also known as Lady Louise Mountbatten, was born on July 13, 1889, in Heiligenberg near Darmstadt, Hesse, Germany, and died on March 7, 1965, in Stockholm. From 1923 to 1950, she was the Crown Princess of Sweden and Duchess of Skåne, and from 1950, she became Queen of Sweden. She was the second wife of King Gustaf VI Adolf.

== Childhood and Youth ==

Princess Louise of Battenberg was the daughter of Prince Louis of Battenberg and Princess Victoria of Hesse, and a great-granddaughter of Queen Victoria of Great Britain. She was also the aunt of Prince Philip, Duke of Edinburgh (the consort of Queen Elizabeth II of England and father of the current King Charles III of England), the sister of Lord Mountbatten, and the niece of Empress Alexandra Feodorovna (who was the wife of Emperor Nicholas II).

Louise was born in Germany but grew up in England. Her father served as an admiral and First Lord of the Admiralty in the British Navy for several years. Her parents had a happy marriage, and the family was described as harmonious. Louise was particularly close to her youngest brother, and they exchanged letters until her death in 1965. Due to her father's work, the family moved between various naval bases in Britain, including a period on Malta. Louise also frequently visited Queen Victoria at Osborne House on the Isle of Wight. However, the family's constant base between these locations was the Hessian summer palace of Heiligenberg near Darmstadt. Louise and her sister were educated by a governess but also attended Texters School for Girls in Darmstadt for a time. In 1909, she received a marriage proposal from King Manuel of Portugal. Edward VII tried to persuade her to accept, but Louise declined because she did not want to enter an arranged marriage. At the same time, she was secretly engaged to Prince Christopher of Greece, but the engagement was broken off because the couple lacked funds, and their parents were unwilling to provide financial support.

In 1914, Louise and her mother were visiting relatives in the Russian Tsarist family when World War I broke out. They had been invited on a river cruise on the Volga aboard the imperial riverboat. During the visit, Louise reportedly observed with concern Rasputin's influence. When the war began, they received a telegram from Louise's father instructing them to leave the country immediately. The river cruise was interrupted, and Louise and her mother became the last non-Russian relatives to see the Tsarist family before their deaths. Louise's mother entrusted her jewels to the Tsarina for safekeeping during the war; these subsequently disappeared after the Russian Revolution. The two women then traveled by boat via Hapsal (now Rapla) in Estonia to neutral territory in Stockholm, Sweden. The boat fare was paid in gold because their currency (German Reichsmarks) was no longer accepted in Russia. They stayed only one day in Sweden, sleeping as guests of the Crown Prince couple at Drottningholm, before returning to England.

Despite considering themselves English and living in England, Louise's father was forced to resign from his position in 1914 due to widespread anti-German sentiment during World War I. During the war, Louise first worked for the Soldiers and Sailors Families Association, then for "Smokes for Soldiers and Sailors." She subsequently volunteered with the Red Cross and served as a nurse from March 1915 to July 1917, initially at a French military hospital in Nevers and later at a wartime hospital near Montpellier. Louise is said to have done excellent work and was awarded The British War and Victory Medals, the Royal Red Cross medal from the British Red Cross, and the French decoration "Médaille de la Reconnaissance française."

With the proclamation of July 17, 1917, when the British royal family adopted the name Windsor, the surname Battenberg was anglicized to Mountbatten, and the head of the family was granted the titles Marquess of Milford Haven, Earl of Medina, and Viscount Alderney. Her father had to renounce all German titles and change the German family name Battenberg to the more English-sounding Mountbatten. Princess Louise of Battenberg thus became "Lady Louise Mountbatten," a name she used until her marriage in 1923.

The family lost their fortune during the war because it was invested in Russian securities and assets. After the war, the family moved to a small house in Fishponds near Southampton. Louise was actively involved in charitable work to help children in the deprived areas of Battersea in London.

== Wedding ==

Her father, on his deathbed, had said that the Crown Prince of Sweden was probably the only one who could be right for his daughter Louise, and that she was the only one who could be right for him. A year or so after his death, the then-Queen Mary brought them together at a horse race. Through Queen Victoria of Great Britain, Louise belonged to the same family circle as the Crown Prince's deceased wife, Margareta. (Louise's uncle was married to Margareta's aunt, and another of Margareta's aunts was the grandmother of Louise.) Gustaf Adolf and Louise had...

How certain are we about this?

People from the 20th century are extremely well-documented. Uncertainty rarely concerns facts themselves, but rather interpretation and evaluation, which can change over time and depending on the observer.
tldr: Louise of Battenberg was a princess who became Queen of Sweden in 1950. She was known for her work as a nurse during World War I and for her role within the royal family.
lattlast:
Louise was born in Germany but grew up in England. She came from a very aristocratic family with relatives in both the British and Russian royal families. During World War I, she worked as a nurse in hospitals to help soldiers, for which she received several awards for her service.

During the war, Louise experienced dramatic events in Russia and was present when the Russian Tsarist family died. After the war, she moved to a smaller house in England because the family's money had disappeared. She then worked extensively with charitable organizations to help children living in poverty.

In 1923, she married the Crown Prince of Sweden, Gustaf Adolf. He later became King, and Louise became Queen in 1950. She lived in Sweden until her death in 1965 and was an important figure in the Swedish royal family for many years.
